edges.description: Haploinsufficiency of dosage-sensitive neurodevelopmental genes (including TCF4) perturbs brain development.
Loss of one MBP copy reduces myelin basic protein dosage at 18q23.
TSHZ1 haploinsufficiency disrupts external/middle-ear and palate development.
Loss of 18q22.3-q23 genes contributes to congenital heart defects.
Growth hormone deficiency from haploinsufficiency of 18q genes contributes to short stature.
Persistent neurodevelopmental disruption causes intellectual disability.
Developmental neurobiologic disruption presents as global delay.
Altered brain development contributes to autism-spectrum and behavioral features.
Reduced MBP dosage is associated with abnormal central white-matter signal.
TSHZ1 dosage loss disrupts external auditory canal development.
edges.target: Atresia of the external auditory canal

edges.description: 18q22.3 dosage loss contributes to palatal clefting and high-arched palate.
Loss of distal 18q cardiac developmental genes produces congenital heart defects.
Growth hormone deficiency and skeletal factors produce short stature.

nodes.description: Heterozygous terminal or interstitial deletion of the long arm of chromosome 18 removes one copy of a contiguous set of genes, producing a haploinsufficiency syndrome. The clinical spectrum reflects which dosage-sensitive genes fall within the deleted interval, so the phenotype varies with breakpoint position and deletion size. The syndrome is conventionally split into a common distal (terminal) form and a rarer proximal interstitial form (18q11.2-q21.1).

evidence.snippet: 18q- syndrome is a rare chromosomal disease caused by the deletion of the long arm of chromosome 18.
Chromosome 18q deletion syndrome is commonly classified into distal deletion and a much rarer proximal interstitial deletion spanning the 18q11.2-q21.1 region.

nodes.description: Abnormal cerebral white-matter MRI signal, historically attributed to reduced myelination from MBP haploinsufficiency, is a characteristic neuroimaging finding.

evidence.snippet: CAA is frequently seen in individuals with an 18q deletion, which is characterized by intellectual disability, reduced white-matter myelination, foot deformities, and distinctive facial features.

nodes.description: Congenital aural atresia or stenosis of the external auditory canal is a characteristic structural ear anomaly underlying much of the conductive hearing loss, attributable to TSHZ1 haploinsufficiency.
nodes.id: Atresia of the external auditory canal

evidence.snippet: CAA is frequently seen in individuals with an 18q deletion, which is characterized by intellectual disability, reduced white-matter myelination, foot deformities, and distinctive facial features.

nodes.description: TSHZ1, in the 18q22.3 critical region, is required for middle-ear and palate development. TSHZ1 haploinsufficiency disrupts development of the external and middle ear, producing congenital aural atresia and conductive hearing loss, and contributes to palatal and midface anomalies.

evidence.snippet: these results demonstrate that hemizygosity of TSHZ1 leads to congenital aural atresia as a result of haploinsufficiency.
gene deficient mouse models for Sall3 or Tshz1 genes, which are located at the 18q22.3 critical region, displayed palate abnormality phenotype.

nodes.description: Autism-spectrum features, attention deficit and hyperactivity, and other behavioral problems are reported.

evidence.snippet: speech delay/autism/attention deficit and hyperactivity/other behavioral problems (30%)

nodes.description: Congenital heart defects occur in roughly a quarter to a third of affected individuals, with a shared distal 18q22.3-q23 overlap region; the most common lesions are pulmonary valve anomalies and atrial septal defects. In proximal deletions, cardiac defects often map distal to GATA6, implying an alternative mechanism rather than GATA6 haploinsufficiency.

evidence.snippet: All 19 individuals shared a small overlapping deletion region between 18q22.3q23. The most common cardiac defects detected were pulmonary valve anomalies and atrial septal defects.

The deletion in four out of five cases with cardiac defect was distal to GATA6, suggesting an alternative mechanism other than haploinsufficiency of GATA6 as an underlying cause of cardiac malformations.

nodes.description: Cleft palate with or without cleft lip is a characteristic craniofacial feature, occurring in about a quarter of affected individuals (a higher fraction when high-arched palate is also counted).

evidence.snippet: The frequency of CP/L and CL among 18q- individuals is about 25%; when high/arched palate cases are included, the frequency rises to about 43%.

nodes.description: Congenital heart defects, most commonly pulmonary valve anomalies and atrial septal defects, occur in a substantial minority of affected individuals.

evidence.snippet: Individuals with the 18q deletion syndrome are presented with various clinical characteristics, including cardiac anomalies in 24-36% of the reported cases.

nodes.description: Developmental delay is present in the large majority of affected individuals.

evidence.snippet: Common presentations of 18q11-q12 deletions include developmental delay/intellectual disability (DD/ID) (82%)

nodes.description: Short stature is recurrent and frequently associated with growth hormone deficiency, with additional contributions from hypothyroidism and skeletal/feeding factors. Growth hormone deficiency is listed among the characteristic features of the syndrome, and affected individuals respond to recombinant human growth hormone.

evidence.snippet: The 18q deletion syndrome (18q-) is a multiple-anomaly disorder associated with mental retardation, white matter anomalies in the brain, growth hormone deficiency, congenital aural atresia, orofacial cleft (OFC), and palate abnormalities.
Some cases with 18q- syndrome can be combined with growth hormone deficiency (GHD)

nodes.description: Intellectual disability of variable severity is a core feature; severity broadly correlates with deletion size and content.

evidence.snippet: Common presentations of 18q11-q12 deletions include developmental delay/intellectual disability (DD/ID) (82%)
The main clinical manifestations of 18q- syndrome include characteristic appearance, intellectual disability, and abnormal genital development.

nodes.description: Deletion of 18q neurodevelopmental genes produces developmental delay, intellectual disability, and behavioral abnormalities. TCF4 haploinsufficiency is a major contributor when the 18q21 region is included (full TCF4 deletion causes Pitt-Hopkins syndrome).

evidence.snippet: Common presentations of 18q11-q12 deletions include developmental delay/intellectual disability (DD/ID) (82%); speech delay/autism/attention deficit and hyperactivity/other behavioral problems (30%); conotruncal heart defects (15%)

nodes.description: The distal 18q region (18q23) includes MBP, encoding myelin basic protein. 18q deletion is classically associated with reduced white-matter myelination and abnormal cerebral white-matter MRI signal. Note that the mechanistic basis is debated: a single autopsy study of a ring-18 patient with confirmed MBP haploinsufficiency found intact myelin and attributed the MRI signal to gliosis rather than dysmyelination (see discussions).

evidence.snippet: CAA is frequently seen in individuals with an 18q deletion, which is characterized by intellectual disability, reduced white-matter myelination, foot deformities, and distinctive facial features.
this syndrome involves the deletion of the myelin basic protein (MBP) gene in 18q23

nodes.description: Short stature is common and frequently associated with growth hormone deficiency that responds to growth hormone therapy.

evidence.snippet: The main features of the syndrome are short stature, hearing loss, hypotonia, mental retardation, endocrine disorders and autoimmunity.
